title: Why the return of spontaneity requires tolerating uncertainty
author: Kalesh

Spontaneity is a luxury you lost for a reason

htmlContent:
Spontaneity is a luxury you lost for a reason

I remember what it felt like to just say yes. To get in a car with friends and drive somewhere without checking the route three times. To laugh at a joke without scanning the room for threats. That person feels like a stranger now. And honestly, I grieve them sometimes.

Here's the thing about complex trauma. It steals spontaneity before you even realize it's gone. Your nervous system learns that safety requires prediction. That survival depends on knowing what's coming next. And it's right. When you grew up in chaos, unpredictability meant danger. A sudden loud voice meant a beating. A change in someone's mood meant abandonment. Your brain got very, very good at scanning for threats because that skill kept you alive.

But now you're an adult. And that same skill is keeping you trapped.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body as frozen energy. Your nervous system gets stuck in a state of hypervigilance, constantly bracing for impact. And spontaneity requires the opposite. It requires relaxation. It requires a felt sense that the world is mostly safe enough to take risks. If your body doesn't believe that, you can't just talk yourself into being spontaneous. You have to address the underlying threat detection system.

I notice this in my own life all the time. Someone invites me to do something unplanned and my first response is always no. Always. Even if I want to say yes. There's this split second where my throat tightens and my stomach drops and I hear myself say "I need to check my schedule" or "maybe another time." The refusal comes from somewhere deeper than conscious choice. It comes from the part of me that still believes unpredictability will kill me.

Wild, right? That's what unresolved trauma does. It keeps you in a cage you built when you were small and powerless, even though you're not small anymore.

Uncertainty tolerance is a skill your nervous system can learn

Here's where it gets hopeful, but not in a toxic positivity way. Your nervous system can change. It's not fixed. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed that your vagus nerve can be retrained to respond differently to uncertainty. The ventral vagal state, which is the state of social engagement and safety, can be strengthened through practice. But it takes time. And it takes tolerating discomfort.

The process isn't about forcing yourself to be spontaneous. That would be like telling someone with a broken leg to just run a marathon. No. The process is about slowly, carefully expanding your window of tolerance for uncertainty. You start small. You do one thing differently today. You take a different route to the grocery store. You eat something you've never tried before. You sit in silence for thirty seconds without checking your phone. Each tiny act of uncertainty tolerance sends a signal to your nervous system: "We survived that. We're okay."

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It helps you understand that your reactions aren't character flaws, they're adaptations. And once you see them that way, you can start to gently challenge them without shame.

I've been practicing this in small ways. Last week I went to a coffee shop I'd never been to before, without looking at the menu online first. That's tiny. Almost ridiculous to even mention. But for my nervous system, it was a big deal. I sat there with the discomfort of not knowing what I would order. My heart was racing. My palms were sweaty. But I stayed. I ordered something random. And nothing bad happened. That's the kind of data your nervous system needs to start trusting uncertainty again.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did you feel a little flutter of anxiety? Did you think "that's too scary for me"? Or did you feel a small sense of possibility? All of that is information. Your body is telling you exactly where your edge is right now.

Your control strategies are not your enemy, but they are not your future

I want to be really clear about something. The strategies you developed to manage uncertainty, the rigid routines, the obsessive planning, the need to know exactly what's happening at all times, these kept you alive. They were smart. They were necessary. And you don't need to hate them or throw them away.

But you might need to expand beyond them.

Janina Fisher, who wrote about trauma-informed treatment, talks about how parts of us get stuck in protective roles. There's a part that insists on control, on predictability, on safety at all costs. That part is not your enemy. It's a scared child who doesn't know you're an adult now. And when you try to be spontaneous without working with that part, it will fight back. Hard. You'll feel panic. You'll feel physical illness. You'll find yourself canceling plans at the last minute with no explanation.

So the work isn't to silence that part. It's to thank it. And then slowly show it that you can handle a little more uncertainty than it thinks.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because here's the paradox: spontaneity actually requires good boundaries. When you don't have boundaries, you're constantly managing other people's needs and expectations. You can't be spontaneous because you're too busy trying to keep everyone else comfortable. Strong boundaries free up the energy you need to take risks.

Think about it. If you know you can say no to something that feels overwhelming, you can say yes to something that feels exciting. If you don't trust your ability to say no, you'll say no to everything just to stay safe. Boundaries create the container for spontaneity to exist.

I've been working with this part of myself that needs to plan everything. I call her the Planner. She makes lists. She checks weather forecasts. She maps out every possible outcome. And she's exhausting. But when I try to bypass her, she throws a tantrum. So instead, I invite her along. I say "Planner, I know you're scared. I know you want to keep me safe. But we're going to try something new today. And I need you to trust me." Sometimes she cooperates. Sometimes she doesn't. Either way, we practice.

The body has to feel safe before the mind can play

This is the part that the self-help industry gets wrong. They tell you to just take risks. To say yes more. To step out of your comfort zone. And that advice works fine for people whose nervous systems are already regulated. But for those of us with complex trauma, that advice can actually make things worse.

Your body needs to feel safe in a visceral, physical way before your mind can access spontaneity. You can't think your way into feeling safe. You have to feel your way there.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored by Deb Dana, who studies Polyvagal Theory. She offers exercises that help you map your nervous system states and find ways to shift toward ventral vagal safety. It's not about forcing yourself to be calm. It's about noticing what actually helps your body feel safe, and doing more of that.

For me, it's feeling my feet on the ground. It's putting a hand on my chest and breathing slowly. It's noticing the color of the sky. It's these tiny anchors that remind my body that right now, in this moment, I am safe. And from that place of safety, I can start to consider spontaneity.

I don't start with big risks. I start with small ones. I leave my phone in another room for ten minutes. I let a conversation go where it goes without steering it. I choose a restaurant without reading the reviews first. Each of these is a tiny experiment in uncertainty tolerance. And each one builds evidence for my nervous system that unpredictability doesn't always mean danger.

Stay with me here. This is slow work. It's not glamorous. It doesn't make for a good Instagram post. But it's real. And it's the only way I've found to actually reclaim the parts of myself that trauma stole.

What spontaneity actually feels like when you're healing

I want to be honest about what spontaneity looks like in recovery. It's not this carefree, joyful thing that movies show. It's messy. It's awkward. It sometimes looks like saying yes to a coffee date and then spending the whole time slightly nauseous with anxiety. It looks like agreeing to a weekend trip and then needing to take three hours to pack because your nervous system is overwhelmed.

But it also looks like moments of genuine surprise. Like laughing at something unexpected and feeling your whole body relax. Like noticing a bird fly past and actually seeing it instead of being lost in thought. Like having a conversation that goes somewhere you didn't plan and feeling connected to another person.

Those moments are worth the discomfort. They remind you that you're alive. That you're more than your trauma. That there's still a person inside who can be surprised by joy.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach. Because spontaneity also requires accepting yourself exactly as you are, including the parts that are scared and rigid. When you can hold all of yourself with compassion, you don't need to be perfect. You can try and fail and try again without the weight of shame crushing you.

I still struggle with this. I still default to no. I still cancel plans sometimes. I still need to plan my spontaneity, which is kind of funny when you think about it. But I'm also saying yes more than I used to. I'm tolerating more uncertainty. I'm letting myself be surprised.

And that's enough. That's progress. That's healing happening in real time, even when it's slow and imperfect.

So if you're reading this and you feel stuck in your need for control, I see you. I am you. And I want you to know that the return of spontaneity is possible. Not because you force it. But because you slowly, gently teach your nervous system that uncertainty is survivable. One small risk at a time. One deep breath at a time. One moment of choosing yes when every cell in your body screams no.

That's the work. And it's worth doing.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab (paid link)
  • What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry (paid link)
  • Anchored by Deb Dana (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach (paid link)
